topics.title: Abstraction in Java
topics.description: Abstraction means hiding complex implementation details and exposing only what is necessary to the user. In Java it is achieved through two mechanisms: abstract classes (partial implementation, can have state, constructors, concrete methods) and interfaces (pure contract, default methods since Java 8, no state except constants). Abstraction defines WHAT an object does, not HOW it does it — the implementation is hidden behind a contract.
topics.story: Think about driving a car. You interact with the steering wheel, pedals, and gearstick — you don't know how the engine ignites fuel or how the ABS brake system works. The car's complexity is abstracted away. You only need the interface: steer, accelerate, brake. An abstract class is like a car model with some standard parts already installed (engine mount, chassis) but requiring you to add the specific engine and transmission. An interface is like a 'driveable vehicle' contract — it just says there must be a steering wheel and pedals, but says nothing about how they work.
topics.analogy: Abstract class = a house blueprint with some walls already built. You must complete the roof and interior (abstract methods), but the foundation and plumbing are done (concrete methods). Interface = a plug standard (USB-C). Any device that follows the standard (implements the interface) can connect — the standard says nothing about what the device does internally. A laptop and a phone both implement USB-C but do completely different things with it.
topics.memoryAnchor: ACI pick rule — Abstract class = IS-A base (CAN have state + constructors + concrete methods + abstract methods; CANNOT instantiate; use when sharing code AND state). Interface = CAN-DO contract (Java 8+: CAN have default+static methods; CANNOT have instance fields; use for capabilities across unrelated classes). Pick test: Can dogs AND birds both FLY? Interface (capability). Are dogs AND wolves both Animals sharing state? Abstract class. Abstract class gives you a 60-90% built vehicle. Interface gives you a driving contract. You can only extend ONE abstract class but implement MANY interfaces.
topics.firstPrinciples: Abstraction solves the complexity problem. Without it, every client that uses a database connection knows about socket management, connection pooling, TCP timeouts. That knowledge is fragile and duplicated. With abstraction: DataSource interface exposes getConnection() — clients call that and get a Connection. The JDBC driver handles all complexity. Client code is stable; implementation can change freely.
topics.bruteForce: Without abstraction: every class directly implements its own version of logging, database access, serialisation. When the underlying technology changes (from MySQL to PostgreSQL, from Log4j to SLF4J), every class must change. Hundreds of ripple-effect changes across the codebase.
topics.optimizedApproach: With abstraction: code against DataSource, Logger, ObjectMapper interfaces. Implementation classes (HikariCP, Logback, Jackson) change without touching client code. This is the Dependency Inversion Principle. Abstract classes share implementation that is always the same (template methods, state management) while keeping variable parts abstract.
topics.whenToUse: Use abstract class when: (1) multiple related classes share common state and implementation, (2) you want to provide a partial implementation with a template method, (3) the relationship is a true IS-A with common internals. Use interface when: (1) unrelated classes share a capability (Comparable, Serializable), (2) you need multiple 'type' classifications for one class, (3) you want to define a pure API contract. Default rule: favour interfaces; use abstract class when you genuinely need shared state.

examples.title: Abstract Class — Template Method Pattern
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":6,"lineCode":"public final void generate() {","variables":{},"phase":"DEFINE","annotation":"Template method is final — subclasses cannot change the algorithm order. They can only fill in the abstract steps"},{"line":8,"lineCode":"String data = fetchData();","variables":{"this":"SalesReport"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"fetchData() is abstract. JVM dispatches to SalesReport.fetchData() — returns sales data string"},{"line":9,"lineCode":"String body = formatBody(data);","variables":{"data":"Sales Q1..."},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"formatBody() is abstract. JVM dispatches to SalesReport.formatBody() — wraps in [SALES] prefix"},{"line":10,"lineCode":"printHeader();","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"printHeader() is concrete in Report — same for all. No override needed. Prints the date"},{"line":11,"lineCode":"System.out.println(body);","variables":{"body":"[SALES] Sales Q1..."},"phase":"PRINT","annotation":"Prints formatted body from the abstract method implementation"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[generate — final template] --> B[fetchData — abstract]
    A --> C[formatBody — abstract]
    A --> D[printHeader — concrete shared]
    A --> E[printFooter — concrete shared]
    B -->|SalesReport| F[returns sales data]
    B -->|InventoryReport| G[returns inventory data]
    C -->|SalesReport| H[SALES prefix]
    C -->|InventoryReport| I[INVENTORY uppercase]
examples.pseudocode:
TEMPLATE METHOD:
  abstract class defines: algorithm skeleton (final)
  concrete methods: shared behaviour
  abstract methods: variable steps

SUBCLASS provides:
  only the variable steps
  cannot change the algorithm order

ADVANTAGE:
  common logic once in abstract class
  subclasses extend without risk of breaking the sequence
examples.explanation: The Template Method pattern is the textbook use of abstract classes. generate() defines the invariant algorithm (start → data → format → header → body → footer → end). Abstract methods fetchData() and formatBody() are the variable parts — each subclass implements them. The final keyword prevents subclasses from changing the order. printHeader() and printFooter() are shared and can optionally be overridden.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring's AbstractController, JdbcTemplate (update template), and HttpServlet all use Template Method. JUnit's TestCase: setUp() and tearDown() are abstract-like hooks; the framework calls them in order. Android's Activity lifecycle: onCreate(), onStart(), onResume() are the template; developers implement only the hooks they need.

examples.title: Interface — Pure Contract With Default Method
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":11,"lineCode":"default List<T> sorted() {","variables":{},"phase":"DEFINE","annotation":"Default method: defined in the interface. Any implementor gets this for free. It calls getItems() and getComparator() — which dispatch polymorphically to the implementing class"},{"line":12,"lineCode":"List<T> copy = new ArrayList<>(getItems());","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"getItems() is abstract — dispatches to NameList.getItems() or ScoreBoard.getItems(). Defensive copy — original list unchanged"},{"line":13,"lineCode":"copy.sort(getComparator());","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"getComparator() dispatches to the implementor. NameList uses String::compareTo; ScoreBoard uses reverseOrder()"},{"line":40,"lineCode":"System.out.println(nl.sorted());","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"nl is NameList. sorted() resolves via default method in Sortable. Internal calls go to NameList's implementations"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    Sortable -->|default| sorted
    sorted -->|calls abstract| getItems
    sorted -->|calls abstract| getComparator
    NameList -->|implements| getItems_names
    NameList -->|implements| getComparator_alpha
    ScoreBoard -->|implements| getItems_scores
    ScoreBoard -->|implements| getComparator_reverse
examples.pseudocode:
INTERFACE CONTRACT:
  abstract method = must implement
  default method  = provided behaviour (can override)
  static method   = utility, no instance needed

DEFAULT METHOD calls abstract methods:
  sorted() calls getItems() and getComparator()
  dispatched to the concrete class at runtime
  → reusable behaviour without inheritance
examples.explanation: Interfaces in Java 8+ can have default methods — shared implementations using the abstract methods declared in the interface itself. sorted() calls getItems() and getComparator() — both are abstract and dispatched polymorphically at runtime to whatever class implements the interface. This gives you shared behaviour without inheritance, resolving the classic 'multiple inheritance of behaviour' problem.
examples.realWorldUse: Java's Comparable.compareTo() enables sort() across all types. Iterable.forEach() default method works for any Iterable. Collection.stream() default method turns any collection into a Stream. java.util.Map.getOrDefault() and computeIfAbsent() are all default methods added in Java 8 without breaking existing Map implementations.

examples.title: Abstract Class vs Interface — When to Choose
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":43,"lineCode":"for (Bird b : birds) {","variables":{"b":"Eagle"},"phase":"LOOP","annotation":"b is a Bird reference. All birds share eat(), breathe() from the abstract class"},{"line":44,"lineCode":"b.eat();","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"eat() is concrete in Bird — same for all. No override. Prints 'Bald is eating'"},{"line":45,"lineCode":"b.makeSound();","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"makeSound() is abstract — dispatches to Eagle.makeSound() → 'Bald: Screech!'"},{"line":46,"lineCode":"if (b instanceof Flyable f) f.fly();","variables":{"f":"Eagle"},"phase":"CHECK","annotation":"Eagle implements Flyable → true. f.fly() dispatches to Eagle.fly(). Penguin not Flyable → skipped"},{"line":47,"lineCode":"if (b instanceof Swimmable s) s.swim();","variables":{},"phase":"CHECK","annotation":"Eagle is NOT Swimmable → skipped. Duck IS → swim() called. Penguin IS → swim() called"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    Bird -->|extends + makeSound abstract| Eagle
    Bird -->|extends + makeSound abstract| Duck
    Bird -->|extends + makeSound abstract| Penguin
    Eagle -->|implements| Flyable
    Duck  -->|implements| Flyable
    Duck  -->|implements| Swimmable
    Penguin -->|implements| Swimmable
examples.pseudocode:
ABSTRACT CLASS (Bird):
  state: name, wingspan
  concrete: eat(), breathe()
  abstract: makeSound()  ← must implement

INTERFACE (Flyable, Swimmable):
  no state, no constructor
  capability contract: fly(), swim()
  any class may implement any combo

COMBO: extends 1 abstract class + implements N interfaces
examples.explanation: Abstract class Bird provides shared state (name, wingspan) and concrete behaviour (eat, breathe) plus the mandatory abstract method makeSound(). Interfaces Flyable and Swimmable represent capabilities — not all birds have both. A class can extend one abstract class but implement many interfaces. This combination is the most powerful abstraction tool in Java.
examples.realWorldUse: Java Collections: AbstractList provides most of the List implementation; you only implement get(int) and size() to have a working List. Spring: AbstractMessageConverter provides content negotiation logic; subclasses only implement readInternal()/writeInternal(). Android: View abstract class + OnClickListener, OnTouchListener interfaces.

examples.title: Functional Interfaces — Abstraction in Lambda Era
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":26,"lineCode":"Validator<Integer> isEven = n -> n % 2 == 0;","variables":{},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Lambda creates an anonymous class implementing Validator<Integer>. isEven holds a reference to the lambda object"},{"line":28,"lineCode":"Validator<Integer> evenAndGt5 = isEven.and(isGtFive);","variables":{},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"and() default method returns a new Validator lambda that calls both. Composition without subclassing"},{"line":20,"lineCode":"for (T item : list) if (v.validate(item)) result.add(item);","variables":{"v":"evenAndGt5 lambda"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"v.validate() dispatches to the lambda's implementation. For evenAndGt5: calls both isEven and isGtFive validators"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    Validator_interface -->|@FunctionalInterface| Lambda
    Lambda -->|isEven| L1[n mod 2 == 0]
    Lambda -->|isGtFive| L2[n > 5]
    Lambda -->|evenAndGt5 = and| L3[L1 AND L2]
    filter -->|calls| validate
    validate -->|dispatches to| L3
examples.pseudocode:
@FunctionalInterface:
  exactly ONE abstract method
  can be instantiated with a lambda
  lambda = anonymous class implementing the interface

COMPOSITION via default methods:
  isEven.and(isGtFive)
  returns new lambda wrapping both
  no subclass needed
examples.explanation: @FunctionalInterface is a single-abstract-method interface that can be instantiated with a lambda. The lambda is syntactic sugar for an anonymous class implementing the interface. Default methods like and() and or() enable composition without subclassing. Built-in: Predicate<T>, Function<T,R>, Consumer<T>, Supplier<T> — all functional interfaces in java.util.function.
examples.realWorldUse: Java Streams API is built entirely on functional interfaces: Stream.filter(Predicate), map(Function), forEach(Consumer), reduce(BinaryOperator). Spring Security: PasswordEncoder functional interface. JPA: Specification<T> for building queries. Mockito: Answer functional interface for mock behaviour customisation.

examples.title: Abstract Class With State — Game Character
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":32,"lineCode":"void performAction(GameCharacter target) {","variables":{"this":"Warrior Arthur"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"performAction is concrete in GameCharacter — shared template. Calls abstract specialMove() and attackPower()"},{"line":33,"lineCode":"String specialMove() — abstract dispatch","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"specialMove() abstract. Dispatches to Warrior.specialMove() → 'Sword Slash'"},{"line":34,"lineCode":"target.takeDamage(attackPower());","variables":{"power":35},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"attackPower() abstract → Warrior.attackPower() = 30+1*5=35. takeDamage(35) concrete — shared damage logic"},{"line":44,"lineCode":"@Override void levelUp()","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"levelUp() abstract. Warrior increments level, strength, maxHealth. Each character type has its own progression"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    GameCharacter -->|abstract| attackPower
    GameCharacter -->|abstract| specialMove
    GameCharacter -->|abstract| levelUp
    GameCharacter -->|concrete| takeDamage
    GameCharacter -->|concrete| performAction
    performAction -->|calls abstract| specialMove
    performAction -->|calls abstract| attackPower
    performAction -->|calls concrete| takeDamage
examples.pseudocode:
ABSTRACT CLASS WITH STATE:
  protected fields: name, health, level
  constructor: initialises shared state
  concrete methods: takeDamage, isAlive, performAction
  abstract methods: attackPower, specialMove, levelUp

SUBCLASS:
  adds its own state (strength, mana)
  implements abstract methods
  inherits shared state + concrete methods
examples.explanation: Abstract classes shine when subclasses share state. GameCharacter holds name, health, and level — all characters need these. Concrete methods takeDamage() and performAction() work for all characters. Abstract methods attackPower(), specialMove(), levelUp() define what varies. Interface cannot hold name and health fields — that's when you need abstract class.
examples.realWorldUse: JPA's AbstractEntity with id, createdAt, updatedAt — every entity extends it, no duplication. Spring's AbstractMessageConverterMethodArgumentResolver with shared content negotiation logic. Android's Fragment class — abstract lifecycle hooks, concrete fragment manager state. Hibernate's AbstractPersistentCollection — shared tracking state for List, Set, Map proxies.

problems.title: Create an Abstract Vehicle
problems.description: Create abstract class `Vehicle` with fields `make`, `year`, `fuelLevel`, concrete methods `refuel(double)` and `getStatus()`, and abstract method `drive(int km)`. Extend as `Car` and `Truck` — each reduces fuelLevel at a different rate in drive(). Verify both share refuel() without reimplementing it.
problems.pattern: Abstract Class — Shared State and Behaviour
problems.hint1: Vehicle: `protected double fuelLevel = 100;` and `void refuel(double amt) { fuelLevel = Math.min(100, fuelLevel+amt); }`
problems.hint2: Car.drive(km): `fuelLevel -= km * 0.08;` (8L/100km). Truck.drive(km): `fuelLevel -= km * 0.15;`
problems.hint3: abstract void drive(int km); — abstract in Vehicle, implemented in each subclass.
problems.constraints: fuelLevel cannot exceed 100.
problems.editorial: Abstract class holds fuelLevel field and refuel() — shared by all vehicles. drive() is abstract — each subclass defines its own fuel consumption rate. Subclasses inherit refuel() for free — no duplication. This is the primary reason to choose abstract class over interface: shared mutable state.

problems.title: Interface With Default Method
problems.description: Create interface `Greetable` with abstract `String getName()` and default method `greet()` that prints `'Hello, I am {getName()}'`. Implement `Student` and `Teacher` classes. Verify that both get the greet() behaviour without duplicating the print logic.
problems.hint1: `interface Greetable { String getName(); default void greet() { System.out.println("Hello, I am " + getName()); } }`
problems.hint2: Student and Teacher only need to implement getName().
problems.hint3: The default greet() calls getName() polymorphically at runtime.
problems.constraints: greet() defined only in interface.
problems.editorial: The default method greet() is written once in the interface and calls getName() — an abstract method. At runtime, getName() dispatches to the implementing class. Both Student and Teacher get greet() for free without code duplication.

problems.title: Cannot Instantiate Abstract Class
problems.description: Demonstrate that trying `new Animal()` where Animal is abstract gives a compile error. Show the correct approach: create a concrete subclass `Dog` that provides the implementation. Return the error message (as a String for this exercise) when you try to conceptually instantiate the abstract.
problems.hint1: Attempting `new Animal()` gives: `Animal is abstract; cannot be instantiated`
problems.hint2: Correct: `Animal a = new Dog();` — Dog provides all abstract method implementations.
problems.hint3: Anonymous subclass: `Animal a = new Animal() { void sound() { ... } };` — this works too.
problems.constraints: Cannot instantiate abstract Animal directly.
problems.editorial: Abstract classes cannot be instantiated directly — they represent incomplete blueprints. The JVM would not know which implementation to use for abstract methods. Concrete subclasses or anonymous subclasses (providing all abstract implementations) can be instantiated.

problems.title: Interface Static Method
problems.description: Create interface `MathUtils` with static method `clamp(int value, int min, int max)` returning the value clamped to [min, max]. Add static method `lerp(double a, double b, double t)` for linear interpolation. Show these are called on the interface directly, not on an instance.
problems.hint1: `interface MathUtils { static int clamp(int v, int min, int max) { return Math.max(min, Math.min(max, v)); } }`
problems.hint2: lerp: `return a + (b - a) * t;`
problems.hint3: Call: `MathUtils.clamp(15, 0, 10)` — on the interface name, not on an instance.
problems.constraints: Must call on interface type, not on instance.
problems.editorial: Interface static methods (Java 8+) are utility functions tightly related to the interface concept. They cannot be overridden by implementing classes (unlike default methods). Called via InterfaceName.method(). Used in Comparator.comparing(), Predicate.not(), Collections.unmodifiableList().

problems.title: Abstract Method Forces Subclass Implementation
problems.description: Create abstract `Validator` with abstract `boolean isValid(String input)` and concrete `validate(String input)` that calls isValid() and prints a result message. Create `EmailValidator` and `PhoneValidator` that each implement isValid() with their own logic. Demonstrate that forgetting to implement isValid() in a concrete class gives a compile error.
problems.hint1: validate() template: `if (isValid(input)) print valid else print invalid`.
problems.hint2: EmailValidator.isValid(): `return input.contains("@") && input.contains(".")`
problems.hint3: PhoneValidator.isValid(): `return input.matches("\\d{10}")`
problems.constraints: No regex for email — use contains().
problems.editorial: Abstract method isValid() forces every concrete subclass to provide validation logic. validate() in the abstract class is the shared template — calls isValid() polymorphically. Forgetting to implement isValid() in a concrete subclass → compile error: 'class EmailValidator is not abstract and does not override abstract method isValid()'.

problems.title: Abstract Class Constructor Chain
problems.description: Create abstract `Account(String id, double balance)` with concrete `deposit()`, `withdraw()`, and abstract `interestRate()`, `calculateInterest()`. Extend as `SavingsAccount` and `CheckingAccount` — each with different interest logic. Show that both call super(id, balance) in their constructors.
problems.hint1: SavingsAccount: `SavingsAccount(String id, double b) { super(id, b); }`
problems.hint2: SavingsAccount.interestRate() = 0.05 (5%). calculateInterest() = balance * interestRate().
problems.hint3: Abstract class constructors cannot be called directly — only via super() from subclass.
problems.constraints: super(id, balance) must be called in each subclass constructor.
problems.editorial: Abstract classes can have constructors — called via super() from subclass constructors. This initialises shared state (id, balance) in one place. deposit() and withdraw() are shared concrete methods. interestRate() and calculateInterest() are abstract — each account type defines its own rates.

problems.title: Implement Comparable — Natural Order Abstraction
problems.description: Create `Employee(String name, double salary, String department)` implementing `Comparable<Employee>`. Natural order: by salary descending. Override equals/hashCode consistently. Sort a list and verify the order. Also demonstrate `Comparator` for an alternate ordering by name.
problems.hint1: `compareTo`: `return Double.compare(other.salary, this.salary)` for descending.
problems.hint2: Comparator by name: `Comparator.comparing(e -> e.name)`
problems.hint3: Collections.sort() uses compareTo. list.sort(byName) uses the Comparator.
problems.constraints: Comparable defines natural order.
problems.editorial: Comparable<T> defines the natural ordering — used by Collections.sort(), Arrays.sort(), TreeSet, and TreeMap without extra arguments. Comparator provides external/alternate orderings. The contract: compareTo returns negative if this < other, 0 if equal, positive if this > other.

problems.title: Abstract Factory — Product Creation
problems.description: Create abstract `UIFactory` with abstract `createButton()` and `createTextField()` returning `Button` and `TextField` interfaces. Implement `DarkThemeFactory` and `LightThemeFactory`. Show that switching the factory changes the entire UI theme without modifying client code.
problems.hint1: interface Button { void render(); } interface TextField { void render(); }
problems.hint2: DarkButton implements Button: `render() → print 'Dark Button [■]'`
problems.hint3: Client method: `renderUI(UIFactory factory)` — creates and renders both components.
problems.constraints: renderUI() must not reference DarkThemeFactory or LightThemeFactory directly.
problems.editorial: Abstract Factory: an abstract class defining the 'factory contract' — what families of related objects to create. Concrete factories (Dark, Light) create matching sets of components. Client code depends only on UIFactory + interfaces — switching themes means swapping the factory. Used in JDBC (DriverManager creates Connection, Statement, ResultSet for each database), AWT/Swing L&F.

problems.title: Multiple Interface Implementation
problems.description: Create interfaces `Saveable` (with `save()`) and `Printable` (with `print()`). Create class `Document` that implements both. Show that Document can be stored in `List<Saveable>` and `List<Printable>` separately, and that processing both lists calls the correct method.
problems.hint1: Document implements Saveable, Printable.
problems.hint2: Store same documents in both List<Saveable> and List<Printable>.
problems.hint3: Iterate each list calling the respective interface method.
problems.constraints: The same Document objects in both lists.
problems.editorial: One object (Document) satisfies multiple interface types. It can be stored and processed as Saveable or Printable — each list sees only the methods of its interface. The same physical objects are viewed through different lenses. This is the power of multiple interface implementation.

problems.title: Abstract Class Preventing Wrong Instantiation
problems.description: Create abstract `DatabaseConnection` with abstract `connect()`, `disconnect()`, and concrete `executeQuery(String sql)` that validates sql and calls connect(). Extend as `MySQLConnection` and `PostgreSQLConnection`. Show that code written against the abstract type works for both databases.
problems.hint1: executeQuery: validate sql not blank, print 'Executing on {dbType}: {sql}', call connect().
problems.hint2: MySQLConnection.connect(): print 'Connected to MySQL'.
problems.hint3: Store both in `DatabaseConnection[]` and call executeQuery polymorphically.

testCases.output:
Executing on MySQL: SELECT 1
Connected to MySQL

Executing on PostgreSQL: SELECT 1
Connected to PostgreSQL

problems.constraints: executeQuery defined only in abstract class.
problems.editorial: executeQuery contains shared validation and logging. connect() is abstract — each database has its own connection logic. Code iterating DatabaseConnection[] works for any database without knowing the concrete type. This is how JDBC's java.sql.Connection abstraction works — application code is database-agnostic.

problems.title: Sealed Interface — Restricted Abstraction
problems.description: Define `sealed interface Shape permits Circle, Rectangle, Triangle`. Each record implements Shape. Use pattern matching switch in `describe(Shape s)` for an exhaustive match. Show the compiler ensures all cases are covered.
problems.hint1: `sealed interface Shape permits Circle, Rectangle, Triangle {}`
problems.hint2: Records: `record Circle(double r) implements Shape {}`
problems.hint3: Switch: `switch (s) { case Circle c -> ...; case Rectangle r -> ...; case Triangle t -> ...; }` — no default needed.
problems.constraints: Java 17+. No default case in switch.
problems.editorial: Sealed interfaces restrict which classes can implement them. The compiler knows all permitted subtypes, making the switch exhaustive — no default needed (compile error if a case is missing). This combines abstraction (interface contract) with closed-world assumption (finite subtypes). Used for algebraic data types, AST nodes.

problems.title: Layered Abstraction — Service Over Repository
problems.description: Create interface `UserRepository` with `findById(int)` and `save(User)`. Create `InMemoryUserRepository` implementing it. Create abstract class `UserService` that takes a UserRepository in its constructor and has concrete `getUser(int id)` and abstract `processUser(User u)`. Implement `ReportUserService` with a specific processUser implementation.
problems.hint1: UserService constructor: `UserService(UserRepository repo) { this.repo = repo; }`
problems.hint2: getUser(): `User u = repo.findById(id); processUser(u); return u;`
problems.hint3: ReportUserService.processUser(): log the user with their details.
problems.constraints: UserService must use UserRepository interface, not InMemoryUserRepository.
problems.editorial: Layered abstraction: UserRepository hides storage details (in-memory, database). UserService hides business logic and depends only on the interface. ReportUserService provides the specific action. Swapping InMemoryUserRepository for a JPA repository requires no changes to UserService or ReportUserService. This is Spring's architecture pattern exactly.

problems.title: Interface Evolution — Adding Default Methods
problems.description: Simulate interface evolution: original `Logger` interface with `log(String msg)`. Ten classes already implement it. Show that adding a `default void logError(String msg)` and `default void logInfo(String msg)` to Logger does NOT break existing implementations. Create two existing classes and one new class that overrides logError.
problems.hint1: Add `default void logError(String msg) { log('[ERROR] ' + msg); }` to Logger.
problems.hint2: Existing SimpleLogger just implements `log(String)` — it inherits logError() and logInfo() automatically.
problems.hint3: AdvancedLogger overrides logError() with a custom format.
problems.constraints: SimpleLogger is unchanged — no new methods added to it.
problems.editorial: Default methods solve the interface evolution problem. Before Java 8, adding a method to an interface broke all existing implementations. Default methods provide a fallback — existing classes get the default behaviour automatically. New classes can override for custom behaviour. This is how Java added forEach() to Iterable and stream() to Collection without breaking all existing implementations.

problems.title: Abstract Builder With Validation
problems.description: Create abstract `Builder<T>` with final `build()` that calls abstract `validate()` first, then `construct()`. `validate()` throws if required fields are missing. `construct()` creates the object. Implement `PersonBuilder` and `CarBuilder`. Show that build() always validates before constructing.
problems.hint1: abstract class Builder<T> { final T build() { validate(); return construct(); } abstract void validate(); abstract T construct(); }
problems.hint2: PersonBuilder.validate(): check name and age. construct(): return new Person(name, age).
problems.hint3: Try building without name — validate() throws IllegalStateException.
problems.constraints: build() is final in Builder — cannot be overridden.
problems.editorial: Abstract Builder with Template Method: build() is the invariant algorithm (validate then construct). Subclasses provide the variable parts. Making build() final ensures validation always runs — no subclass can bypass it. This guarantees safety across all builder types.

problems.title: Abstract Iterator
problems.description: Create abstract `AbstractIterator<T>` implementing `java.util.Iterator<T>`. Provide concrete `hasNext()` using an abstract `int currentIndex()` and `int totalSize()`. Subclasses implement `T getElement(int index)`, `totalSize()`, and a mutable `currentIndex`. Show both `ArrayIterator` and `RangeIterator` work through the same abstract base.
problems.hint1: hasNext(): `return currentIndex() < totalSize();`
problems.hint2: next(): `return getElement(currentIndex());` then increment.
problems.hint3: ArrayIterator: wraps T[], currentIndex is a field. RangeIterator: iterates from start to end inclusive.
problems.constraints: AbstractIterator must not know about the concrete data source.
problems.editorial: Abstract Iterator: hasNext() and next() are the template — subclasses provide totalSize(), currentIndex(), advanceAndGet(), getElement(). The abstract class handles the iteration contract; each subclass handles its own data source. This is how AbstractList provides iterator() for any indexed list.

problems.title: Diamond Problem — Interface Default Conflict
problems.description: Create interfaces `A` and `B` both with `default String hello() { return "Hello from A/B"; }`. Interface `C` extends both A and B. Show the conflict when a class implements C. Resolve it by overriding hello() in the implementing class. Also show resolution by calling `A.super.hello()`.
problems.hint1: class Impl implements C must override hello() — compile error otherwise.
problems.hint2: Return `A.super.hello() + " and " + B.super.hello()` to use both.
problems.hint3: If C extends both A and B and C has its own hello(), C wins for Impl (class over interface).

testCases.output: Hello from A and Hello from B

problems.constraints: C itself does not override hello().
problems.editorial: Diamond problem: A and B both provide hello(). When Impl implements C (which extends both), the compiler cannot decide which default to use — it forces an explicit override. `A.super.hello()` explicitly calls A's default, bypassing the diamond ambiguity. This is Java 8's explicit solution: conflicts must be resolved by the class, never silently.

problems.title: Generic Abstract Class
problems.description: Create generic abstract class `Repository<T, ID>` with abstract `save(T entity)`, `findById(ID id)`, `delete(ID id)`, and concrete `saveAll(List<T> entities)` that loops and calls save(). Implement `InMemoryProductRepository` and `InMemoryOrderRepository`. Verify saveAll() works for both without reimplementing.
problems.hint1: abstract class Repository<T, ID> { abstract void save(T e); abstract Optional<T> findById(ID id); void saveAll(List<T> list) { list.forEach(this::save); } }
problems.hint2: InMemoryProductRepository extends Repository<Product, Integer> using a HashMap<Integer, Product>.
problems.hint3: saveAll() calls save() polymorphically — each repository's save() is different.
problems.constraints: saveAll() defined only in abstract class.
problems.editorial: Generic abstract Repository provides saveAll() once for all repositories. Each concrete repository implements save(), findById(), delete() for its specific type. Type safety is enforced by generics — ProductRepository can never save an Order. This is Spring Data JPA's CrudRepository architecture in miniature.

problems.title: Abstract Class and Interface Combination
problems.description: Design a payment system: interface `Auditable` with `String getAuditLog()`. Abstract class `Payment` with state (amount, currency), concrete `getReceipt()`, and abstract `process()`. `CreditCardPayment` extends Payment and implements Auditable. Show it can be processed as Payment and audited as Auditable.
problems.hint1: abstract class Payment { double amount; String currency; abstract void process(); String getReceipt() { return String.format('%s %.2f', currency, amount); } }
problems.hint2: CreditCardPayment extends Payment implements Auditable.
problems.hint3: Store as Payment for processing, as Auditable for audit — same object.
problems.constraints: Same object used as Payment and Auditable.
problems.editorial: Combining abstract class and interface: Payment holds state (amount, currency) and shared concrete method (getReceipt). CreditCardPayment implements the abstract process() and adds the Auditable capability. The same object is usable through both types. This is how Spring's @Transactional works — a service is both a business component (extends AbstractService) and a TransactionCapable (implements the proxy interface).

problems.title: Abstract Recursive Structure — File System
problems.description: Model a file system: abstract class `FileSystemEntry` with `name`, `size()`, `toString(String indent)`. `File` is a leaf with fixed size. `Directory` contains a List<FileSystemEntry> and size() = sum of children's sizes. Print the tree showing sizes.
problems.hint1: abstract class FileSystemEntry { String name; abstract long size(); abstract void print(String indent); }
problems.hint2: File extends FileSystemEntry: size() returns its bytes. Directory: size() = children.stream().mapToLong(FileSystemEntry::size).sum()
problems.hint3: Directory.print(): prints self then iterates children calling child.print(indent+"  ")

testCases.output:
root/ (7680 bytes)
  readme.txt (1024 bytes)
  src/ (6144 bytes)
    Main.java (4096 bytes)
    Utils.java (2048 bytes)
  pom.xml (512 bytes)

problems.constraints: No instanceof in size() or print().
problems.editorial: Composite pattern via abstract class: File and Directory both extend FileSystemEntry. Directory.size() recursively calls size() on children polymorphically — File returns bytes, Directory sums recursively. No instanceof needed — each type knows its own size computation. Identical to java.io.File (length() works differently for files vs dirs).

problems.title: Fluent Abstract Builder Chain
problems.description: Create a fluent abstract builder where `AbstractBuilder<B, T>` provides `self()` returning type B (for return type covariance) and final `build()`. `PersonBuilder` and `EmployeeBuilder` (which extends PersonBuilder) use this to enable fluent chains that return the correct concrete builder type without casting.
problems.pattern: Fluent Generic Builder with Self Type
problems.hint1: `abstract class AbstractBuilder<B extends AbstractBuilder<B,T>, T> { abstract B self(); final T build() { validate(); return construct(); } }`
problems.hint2: PersonBuilder<B extends PersonBuilder<B>> extends AbstractBuilder<B,Person>. self() returns (B) this.
problems.hint3: EmployeeBuilder extends PersonBuilder<EmployeeBuilder> — the chain is fully typed.
problems.constraints: No casting when calling name().age() on EmployeeBuilder.
problems.editorial: Fluent builder with self type: `B extends AbstractBuilder<B,T>` is a recursive generic bound — ensures self() returns the concrete builder type. name() and age() return B (the concrete type) — so on EmployeeBuilder they return EmployeeBuilder, enabling fluent chaining with company() without casting. Used in Lombok @Builder, Protobuf builders, and test data factories.
